title: Open Source AI: Use It, Own It

modules.title: What Open Source AI Actually Means and Why It's a Big Deal
modules.sub: Unpacking what open source really means in the AI world
modules.description: Learners cut through the buzzwords and build a clear definition of open source AI, distinguishing it from proprietary alternatives. They examine real examples of open source models and ask why access, transparency, and community ownership matter. By the end, they can explain to someone else why this movement is reshaping who gets to shape AI.

modules.title: The Landscape: Key Models, Tools, and Communities to Know
modules.sub: Mapping the major players beyond OpenAI and Google
modules.description: Learners survey the open source AI ecosystem, getting familiar with landmark models like LLaMA and Mistral, key platforms like Hugging Face, and the communities driving development. They compare tools across use cases and start forming opinions about which projects align with their own interests. The goal is a working mental map they can actually navigate.

modules.title: Running AI Locally: What You Need and How to Get Started
modules.sub: Getting real AI models running on your own machine
modules.description: Learners assess their own hardware, choose an appropriate local AI tool such as Ollama or LM Studio, and walk through the process of downloading and running a model on their own device. They troubleshoot common setup issues and experience firsthand what it means to run AI without sending data to an external server. This module turns theory into a working local environment.

modules.title: Customizing and Fine-Tuning Without a PhD
modules.sub: Shaping AI behavior to fit your specific needs
modules.description: Learners explore practical methods for customizing open source models, including prompt engineering, system prompts, and lightweight fine-tuning approaches that don't require deep machine learning expertise. They experiment with adapting a model's tone, focus, or knowledge for a specific task. The module builds confidence that customization is a learnable skill, not a gatekept one.

modules.title: Privacy, Control, and Why Big Tech Doesn't Want You Here
modules.sub: Understanding what you give up with proprietary AI tools
modules.description: Learners analyze the data, privacy, and dependency tradeoffs embedded in using closed AI platforms versus running open source alternatives. They examine real terms of service, consider who benefits from centralized AI infrastructure, and think critically about the power dynamics at play. The module helps learners make informed, deliberate choices about which tools they trust with their work and data.

modules.title: Open Source AI for Your Career: Skills That Get You Hired
modules.sub: Translating open source fluency into real career value
modules.description: Learners identify the specific open source AI skills employers in tech, research, media, and other fields are actively seeking. They audit their own developing skill set and draft a narrative connecting their open source experience to career goals, whether that's a job, freelance work, or a portfolio project. The module makes the link between hands-on experimentation and professional credibility explicit.

modules.title: Contributing to the Community Without Being an Expert
modules.sub: Finding your real role in open source AI projects
modules.description: Learners discover the many ways people contribute to open source AI beyond writing code, including documentation, testing, translation, dataset curation, and community moderation. They explore beginner-friendly projects and identify at least one contribution they could realistically make now. The module reframes contribution as a spectrum and lowers the barrier to genuine participation.

modules.title: Building Something Real: Your First Open Source AI Project
modules.sub: Putting everything together in a project you can show
modules.description: Learners scope, build, and document a small but complete project using open source AI tools, applying skills developed across the course. They make real decisions about which model to use, how to customize it, and how to present their work to others. The finished project becomes a tangible portfolio artifact and a proof point for everything they've learned.

synopsis: You don't have to depend on OpenAI or Google â a massive ecosystem of free, powerful AI tools exists outside Big Tech, and knowing how to use it is becoming a serious career advantage. This course gets you hands-on with open source AI so you can build, experiment, and stay in control of your own work.
rationale: Open source AI fluency is increasingly valued in technical and non-technical roles alike, but it's rarely taught accessibly â leaving young adults dependent on closed platforms they can't customize, audit, or afford at scale.
learning_outcome: Learners can identify and run appropriate open source AI tools for their own projects, understand the tradeoffs versus proprietary options, and describe how this skill set applies to their career goals.
